Your transactions show a $300 contribution to Colorado 529 CollegeInvest in May 2026 — a great start for your child's education future! Since tax and account-structure matters are involved here, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
How your 529 is set up
You're using Colorado 529 CollegeInvest, which is a strong choice for Colorado residents. Contributions to Colorado's plan are deductible on your state income tax return (Colorado allows a deduction for contributions, subject to annual limits), which is a meaningful perk given Colorado's 4.41% flat income tax rate. Your money grows tax-deferred, and qualified withdrawals are completely federal and state tax-free.
Is $300/month enough?
At $300/month, you're on a solid trajectory — but whether it's "enough" depends on your child's age and your target. As a general benchmark, four-year public university costs are currently in the range of $100,000–$130,000 all-in (tuition, room, board), and private universities run $250,000+. If your child is young, compounding does a lot of heavy lifting over 15–18 years, so consistency matters more than the starting amount. If you'd like to model a specific target and timeline, that's a great exercise to do together.
Key tax and flexibility caveats to know
What counts as a qualified expense (federal rules, 2026):
College tuition, fees, books, supplies, and required equipment
Room and board for students enrolled at least half-time (capped at the school's cost of attendance)
K-12 tuition up to $20,000 per beneficiary per year (expanded under the OBBBA for 2026 — up from $10,000)
Apprenticeship program costs registered with the U.S. Department of Labor
Student loan repayment up to a $10,000 lifetime limit per borrower
What triggers taxes and penalties:
Non-qualified withdrawals are subject to ordinary income tax plus a 10% federal penalty on the earnings portion only — your contributions (basis) come out penalty-free since they were already after-tax dollars
Powerful flexibility options:
You can change the beneficiary to another qualifying family member at any time without tax consequences — helpful if your child gets a scholarship or chooses a different path
529-to-Roth IRA rollover (SECURE 2.0): If the account has been open at least 15 years and funds weren't contributed in the last 5 years, you can roll up to $35,000 lifetime (capped at the annual Roth IRA limit of $7,500 in 2026) into your child's Roth IRA — a fantastic safety valve for overfunded accounts
ABLE account rollover: Permanently available if your child has a qualifying disability
FAFSA impact (important): As a parent-owned 529, this account is assessed at no more than 5.64% in the federal financial aid formula — quite favorable. Distributions from a parent-owned 529 are also ignored for FAFSA purposes under post-2024 rules. This is meaningfully better than a student-owned account.
State conformity caveat: Colorado generally conforms to federal 529 rules, but always verify with a Colorado CPA — some expanded uses (like the K-12 increase) may have nuances at the state level.
One area worth watching
Your $300/month contribution is recorded as a one-time transaction in May rather than a recurring automatic transfer. Setting up an automatic monthly contribution directly through CollegeInvest removes the friction of remembering each month and keeps compounding working consistently.
Would you like to explore how different monthly contribution amounts might grow over a specific timeframe for your child's education goal?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
